title: MHRA’s radical rare disease proposal - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Access through your institution

Buy or subscribe

The UK’s regulator is proposing an ambitious new pathway for regulating therapies that treat diseases too rare to be considered commercially viable. The Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) unveiled the Rare Disease Therapies Regulatory Framework, designed to allow flexibility in how evidence is collected and presented to regulators to make it quicker and easier for therapies to gain approval.The new guidance is built to overcome the challenges facing very rare diseases — specifically, the small patient numbers. Ultra-rare diseases are defined as those that affect fewer than 1 in 50,000 people, which makes recruiting the large cohorts required by conventional pathways not only slow but also difficult and expensive. The proposed framework will allow developers flexibility around how they conduct clinical trials, enable changes in how they collect and present the evidence, and make it easier to incorporate surrogate and patient end-points as well as computational modelling, digital twins and non-animal methods.
